name: Totaka
dates_notes: One of the four chief disciples per tradition; the Toṭakāṣṭaka survives.
core_thesis: Toṭaka, traditionally one of Śaṅkara's four chief disciples, is best known through the *Toṭakāṣṭaka* — eight verses of *guru-stuti* in *toṭaka* metre (whence his received name) addressed to Śaṅkara as *Bhagavatpāda*. The hymn is doctrinally compressed but expresses the Advaita *guru-bhakti* register at its purest: the *guru* is the operative locus of *brahma-vidyā*, and surrender to the *guru* — *bhava śaṅkara deśika me śaraṇam* (refrain) — is the seeker's *paramārtha*-fruit, since the *guru*'s teaching is what makes *mahāvākya*-cognition arise. The longer (and substantially longer) *Śrutisāra-samuddharaṇa* — 'Extraction of the Essence of the Vedas' — traditionally ascribed to him is a more discursive verse-treatise summarizing core Advaita doctrine (the four *mahāvākyas*, *adhyāsa*, *avidyā*, the path of *jñāna*) but its ascription is less universally fixed than the *Aṣṭaka*. The figure of Toṭaka is, like Hastāmalaka, principally known through tradition and through this devotional poetry rather than through systematic doctrinal exposition.

engaged_works.title: Totakashtaka
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Traditionally ascribed; received by the Advaita maṭha tradition.
engaged_works.summary: Eight verses (*aṣṭaka*) in *toṭaka* metre — a tetrameter whose name the disciple is traditionally said to have received from the work itself — addressed in the second person to Śaṅkara. Each verse closes with the refrain *bhava śaṅkara deśika me śaraṇam* ('Be, Śaṅkara my preceptor, my refuge'). The verses successively present (i) reverence for Śaṅkara as the embodiment of *brahma-vidyā*, (ii) the disciple's incapacity without the *guru*'s grace, (iii) Śaṅkara's role as dispeller of the *saṃsāra*-illusion, (iv) the *guru* as the ocean of *kāruṇya*, and so on, culminating in the surrender-formula. The hymn is recited liturgically in Advaita *maṭha* contexts and is part of the standard *guru-paramparā* sequence of devotional recitation. Traditionally ascribed; received throughout the Advaita *maṭha* tradition. The longer *Śrutisāra-samuddharaṇa* is also traditionally ascribed to Toṭaka but is not represented as a separate entry in the present skeleton — see report.

engaged_works.title: Shrutisara-Samuddharana
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: If genuinely Toṭaka's, contemporary with Śaṅkara's mature period; some scholars place it later.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Traditionally ascribed to Toṭaka by the Advaita maṭha tradition; some scholars dispute the ascription on stylistic and doctrinal grounds, with proposals ranging from a near-contemporary disciple to a later Advaita pedagogue who took Toṭaka's name.

ascription_evidence.description: Received as Toṭaka's by the Advaita maṭha tradition.
Modern scholars (e.g. Pollock, Mahadevan) raise stylistic and chronological doubts.

engaged_works.summary: A substantial verse-*prakaraṇa* — "Extraction of the Essence of the Vedas" — traditionally ascribed to Toṭaka and treating, in compact verse-paragraphs, the core doctrinal architecture of Advaita: the four *mahāvākyas* with their *jahad-ajahal-lakṣaṇā* analyses, the *adhyāsa* doctrine of superimposition as the universal pre-philosophical condition, the structure of *avidyā* and its relation to *Brahman* and *jīva*, the *jñāna-niṣṭhā* / *karma-niṣṭhā* polarity, and the path of *śravaṇa-manana-nididhyāsana* culminating in *aparokṣa-jñāna*. The work is considerably longer than the *Toṭakāṣṭaka* and serves as a doctrinal complement to that hymn — supplying the systematic content the *Aṣṭaka*'s devotional surrender presupposes. The ascription is traditionally accepted within the Advaita *maṭha* lineages but disputed by parts of modern scholarship: stylistically the verses show some technical formulations more characteristic of post-Sureśvara Advaita than of an immediate Śaṅkara disciple, leading some to propose a later authorship under the same traditional name. The work is in any case the principal Advaita treatise associated with Toṭaka's lineage-figure in the school's living tradition.
